data.title: China Automotive Systems: Some Positive Developments And Some Negative Ones
data.description:
It almost looked like it was finally going to happen for China Automotive Systems Inc. (NASDAQ:CAAS), a supplier of power steering components and systems for the automotive industry, but in the end CAAS was unable to get it done. The stock came close to getting past a price region it has struggled in for quite some time, but in the end the end result was the same as the prior ones. The stock has been kept contained for over a year, and there are additional headwinds on the horizon that could give CAAS more problems. However, there are also positive signs to be found, enough to make sticking with long CAAS worth it. Why will be covered next.

An attempted breakout comes close, but fails in the end

A previous article from last June mentioned how the deteriorating trade relationship between the U.S. and China was a problem for CAAS with the introduction of tariffs, but the article also noted how CAAS could compensate for problems in one market with growth elsewhere. While acknowledging the presence of headwinds, CAAS had a path available in terms of overcoming present headwinds. This argued in favor of long CAAS even if time would be needed, as the headwinds could make gains hard to come by in the short term. Accordingly, I rated CAAS a buy in the article.

The notion that gains may be hard to come by in the short term turned out to be warranted because the stock price is not far from when the previous article was written several months ago. However, CAAS did attempt to do better, but its attempt to rally higher in September got snuffed out because CAAS was unable to get past the $4.60-5.00 price region, something that has happened before in the past 12 months, as shown in the chart above.

The chart shows how the numerous attempts to rally higher ended in the same $4.60-5.00 region. CAAS was able to briefly move above the aforementioned region, either on an intraday basis or for one or two days, but CAAS has not been able to stay above this region for over a year. CAAS is up against resistance it has yet to crack.

However, while the latest failed attempt after all this time may discourage some who are bullish on CAAS, there are a couple of encouraging signs to be found. While the stock was unable to get past resistance, it also did not break the prevailing trend. The lows, for instance, have been going higher, which can be seen if they are connected with a trendline in the chart below. What is worth noting is how the stock bounced after it came close to this trendline, following the decline in the stock after the failed attempt to break out.

If we assume the trendline holds, then this puts CAAS in position for another attempt at breaking out, which, unlike the past attempts in the last 12 months, may fare better. The ascending trendline is converging on the $4.60-5.00 region, which means that one of the two will have to yield at some point down the line. Either possibility has a shot, but, more often than not, the above chart pattern favors a breakout to the upside rather than a breakdown to the downside.

CAAS has seen upwards and downwards pressure

The decision to redomicile CAAS as a Cayman Islands company sent the stock price higher, starting with a 6% gain on September 2, but CAAS has also had to contend with a couple of potentially new headwinds on the horizon, on top of the other ones that have been around all year, automotive tariffs in particular. In fact, the tariff situation in the industry has gotten more convoluted with the U.S. government announcing an additional 25% tariff on medium- and heavy-duty trucks.

CAAS took a hit when Stellantis (STLA) announced on September 22 that it was pausing production at several of its plants due to weak demand, tariffs being a contributing factor. Stellantis was CAAS’ number one customer in FY2024 with over 20% of revenue, which means any struggles at such an important customer could affect CAAS.

The latest headwind for the industry is China’s decision to tighten export controls on rare earth elements. Export restrictions on rare earth had already caused disruptions earlier in the year, but the updated ones have the potential to cause further disruptions to the production of automobiles, particularly in the U.S. and Europe.

Also adding to the sector’s recent woes is the possible disruption in the supply of automotive chips from Nexperia due to a dispute between China and the Netherlands as to who controls Nexperia. The Netherlands took control of the company, which caused China to impose export controls on the company’s local subsidiary and restrictions on chips being send overseas.

Does CAAS stand to be affected by recent export controls on rare earth?

CAAS is a supplier of power steering systems to the automotive industry. It supplies both the traditional ones using hydraulics or hydraulic power steering, but also the more modern ones using electric motors or electric power steering/EPS. The former do not need any components using rare earth, but, while there are ongoing attempts to design EPS without using rare earth, most EPS used today requires neodymium and dysprosium for the permanent magnets in the electric motors.

On paper, this means the latest export controls on rare earth are relevant to CAAS, especially when it comes to countries outside of China. According to the most recent Form 10-K, Stellantis is CAAS’ top customer with 20.3% of FY2024 revenue and the third largest was India’s Mahindra & Mahindra with 6.8%. CAAS derived 31.8% of revenue from countries other than China, including 16.6% from the U.S., which suggests around a third of sales may be disrupted, or at least delayed.

Why the impact could be manageable for CAAS

On the other hand, that still leaves 68.2% in China that should be relatively safe. It is also possible the remaining share will see a negligible impact because export controls do not preclude the export of rare earth, as long as relevant licenses are issued by the Chinese government. If official statements are anything to go by, the Chinese government seems to be targeting the military applications of rare earth and not so much civilian applications.

If this is correct, CAAS should be okay, as its customers are on the civilian side. There might be some delays as companies wait for their licenses, but they should be able to apply for them. The latest round of export controls could turn out like the previous one where the worst case was avoided in the end and business went on as usual.

The most recent, or Q2 FY2025 report did not show much of an impact on CAAS. On the contrary, CAAS actually raised its outlook for FY2025 by forecasting revenue of $720M at the end of the year, up from the previous $700M. FY2024 revenue was $650.94M, which implies FY2025 revenue will grow by 10.7% YoY to $720M, the fifth consecutive year of growth.

CAAS grew revenue by 11.1% YoY to $176.2M in Q2 FY2025, including $103.3M from traditional steering products, and EPS contributed the remaining $72.9M. The latter was up 31.1% YoY, but keep in mind that this is the segment potentially exposed to export rules as mentioned before. Income from operations increased by 20.2% YoY to $13.0M, but EPS increased by just $0.01 YoY to $0.25, mostly due to a doubling in income taxes.

CAAS earned $0.49 a share YTD, or the first two quarters of FY2025, which is worth noting because it is less than the $0.51 earned in the same period a year ago, even though revenue increased by double digits in both quarters. The decrease in net margins was a combination of higher taxes, increased spending on things like research and development, but also higher costs due to tariffs.

Given what has happened this year, the bottom line is likely to see less of an improvement than the top line by the time the year is done. FY2025 revenue may increase by 10.7% YoY to $720M, but my estimate is that FY2025 EPS will see less of an improvement to $1.05, or an increase of about 6% compared to FY2024 EPS of $0.99.

CAAS is worth the risk at current valuations

However, longer term, earnings should grow in line with revenue. CAAS was able to grow revenue at a CAGR in the low teens in the past five years, and CAAS should be able to achieve a CAGR of at least 10% in the next five years. CAAS depends on Chinese carmakers for over two-thirds of sales, and they have been gaining market share at the expense of other automakers CAAS is less exposed to. This should help grow sales at CAAS.

If CAAS is able to grow the top line at a CAGR of 10% in the next five years, and by extension the bottom line by a similar amount, or 10%, then CAAS is arguably undervalued at current levels. TTM EPS totaled $0.97, which accords CAAS a P/E ratio of 4.5x with the stock closing at $4.41 on October 21.

According to the methodology espoused by Peter Lynch, CAAS would be undervalued relative to growth if it grows EPS at a CAGR of 10% in the next five years, or more than twice the 4.5% needed to be considered fairly valued. Fair value for CAAS should be closer to about $10.00 a share, assuming earnings grow at the estimated CAGR of roughly 10% and TTM EPS of $0.97.

Furthermore, CAAS can not only be argued to be undervalued based on the income statement because the same can be said based on the balance sheet. CAAS is available at way less than its book value of $12.14 a share and a stock price of $4.41, which translates to a price-to-book of just 0.36x. At these levels, CAAS is justified to be buying back shares, something it has already been doing.

Investor takeaways

CAAS has been dealing with headwinds all year, which likely played a role in how the stock has struggled to move higher in the past 12 months. CAAS is a supplier to the automotive industry, and the industry has been and continues to be bedeviled by various upheavals, including tariffs on not just the finished product, but also inputs like steel and aluminum.

The industry could face further upheaval in the form of disruptions caused by shortages of automotive chips due to a dispute between the Netherlands and China, but also disruptions to production as a result of China’s decision to tighten export controls on rare earth elements. Rare earth is used in a number of auto parts and components, including the electric power steering systems supplied by CAAS, a segment that has been outperforming for CAAS.

Yet while it would not be correct to state CAAS has not been affected by all the turmoil, CAAS is doing okay from an operational standpoint. CAAS continues to grow, and it can even be argued to be undervalued relative to growth, as detailed earlier. The balance sheet also suggests CAAS deserves a significantly higher stock price.

CAAS has been able to accomplish this because the headwinds have not actually impacted it all that much. This is partly due to the fact that most sales are internal within China and therefore not subject to issues like U.S. tariffs, but also because while there have been disruptions in the automotive industry, they have not turned into the worst case so far.

This is likely to continue even with the updated rare earth controls because China does not seem to be going after the kind of products CAAS deals with. It’s more the military stuff that should really be concerning. If the headwinds that have turned up on the horizon have a minimal impact on CAAS as a result, the income statement and CAAS itself should not be impacted much.

I am thus bullish on CAAS, all things considered. CAAS has been unable to get past resistance for over a year despite several attempts, but it is encouraging to see how CAAS continues to make follow-up attempts to break out. As long as CAAS keeps trying to move higher, odds are CAAS will eventually succeed in a future attempt, given enough time.

data.title: Cracks Emerge Beneath Market Resilience, Challenging Areas Of The U.S. Economy
data.description:
Two recent high-profile bankruptcies of an auto lender and an auto parts company triggered loan loss announcements at a handful of banks. The sudden collapse of these companies has sparked wider concerns.

Since the news, spreads of lower-quality publicly traded debt have widened versus those of similar-maturity U.S. Treasuries. Also, equity prices of regional banks and publicly traded business development companies (BDCs) have come under some pressure, and the credit market has started to price some higher risk premium on certain segments of bank loans and securitized consumer credit products.

While these bankruptcies and losses appear idiosyncratic, these kinds of issues tend to surface when strong economy-wide growth fundamentals fade – as we are seeing now – and struggling companies and households can no longer mask underlying problems.

The economic and policy backdrop in the U.S. – including slower (though still positive) growth, higher tariff-related costs, dramatic immigration policy changes, and still-elevated interest rates – is challenging pockets of the economy, with the effects just beginning to become more visible.

Ultimately, we believe the U.S. economy is in a position to weather these headwinds, especially once the income tax cuts and credits from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act start to provide support to households and businesses. In the overall U.S. economy, household and corporate debt-to-GDP and debt-to-wealth ratios are now stronger than before the pandemic, according to Federal Reserve data.

However, strong aggregate metrics mask a subtle shift toward riskier lending under the surface that poses risks: Post-pandemic credit rating “inflation” and the dramatic growth in private lending have meant that many borrowers who might not otherwise have had access to financing have gotten it.

On top of that, the economic adjustments to recent policy changes will continue to challenge business models. Companies exposed to international trade or that were lifted by the immigration boom are particularly vulnerable. Lower-income households that are not participating in equity market gains or whose labor income is exposed to affected sectors could also face challenges.

The broader markets still appear relatively sanguine about these risks, despite the recent repricing in specific areas of credit and equities.

Backdrop: Strong post-pandemic fundamentals supported riskier lending

How did markets get here? The U.S. economy performed very well in the years following the pandemic. Massive pandemic-related government transfers to households and businesses strengthened private sector balance sheets.

We also saw slower pass-through of higher interest rates to households due to a large pool of long-term fixed-rate mortgage debt, plus an immigration boom bolstering both the supply and demand side of the economy. All these trends contributed to stronger U.S. real GDP performance relative to other developed markets.

This U.S. “exceptionalism” also likely contributed to an expansion in riskier lending, including private lending to riskier small and middle-market businesses. Lenders offered more flexible credit arrangements, including upfront payment-in-kind (PIK) interest payment plans, to manage high interest rates.

Many companies that likely couldn’t get financing in public markets or through direct bank loans secured financing in the private markets, while banks increasingly lent to financial intermediaries such as BDCs instead of underwriting more expensive direct-to-corporate loans.

The net result was a shift in credit from banks and public markets to private lending, which blunted the pass-through of tighter monetary policy into the real economy. According to Federal Reserve financial accounts, economy-wide nonfinancial corporate credit outstanding at 142% of GDP is 7 percentage points (ppts) lower than before the pandemic.

By contrast, private credit, an estimated $2.7 trillion subset of the total corporate debt outstanding, is up almost 1 ppt relative to GDP since before the pandemic. Over the past 10 years, a little over half of the growth in total corporate debt relative to GDP has been associated with private credit.

These dynamics were mirrored in consumer lending as U.S. households also benefited from post-pandemic economic growth. In addition to pandemic-related fiscal supports and student loan forbearance, many households saw stronger wage gains (especially if workers switched jobs), while faster home price appreciation created wealth gains even for households that do not participate in the stock market.

These factors drove a mechanical improvement or “inflation” in U.S. consumers’ credit scores, which on average rose by about 10 to 15 points immediately following the pandemic (according to Equifax), with previously lower-credit-quality borrowers enjoying greater gains.

These inflated credit scores resulted in a shift toward riskier lending despite lower aggregate household debt-to-GDP levels. Since then, delinquency rates for consumer credit products have risen, especially for 2021–2022 vintages issued at the peak of this improvement in credit scores.

Policy changes create vulnerabilities

Over the past year, the post-pandemic U.S. economic exceptionalism has faded – real GDP growth has downshifted from a 2.5%–3% pace to 1.5%–2%, according to the Bureau of Economic Analysis and our estimates.

Material changes in trade and immigration policies are challenging many business models. As companies become convinced that tariffs, taxes, and immigration policies are here to stay, they’ll likely adjust their strategies – some with more success than others.

The recent high-profile bankruptcies and the timing of their collapse are consistent with this changing macro backdrop, although these cases were also allegedly exacerbated by inconsistencies in collateral accounting and pledges.

One of the collapsed companies primarily focused on subprime auto lending to borrowers with short credit or no credit histories and to immigrants who may have been affected by broader U.S. policy changes this year. “Geopolitical uncertainty and headwinds from newly imposed tariffs” were cited as challenges that contributed to the auto parts firm’s bankruptcy.

At the same time, asset quality has come into focus in third-quarter earnings announcements when some banks reported losses relating to these two bankruptcies, as well as smaller issues of missing or mispledged collateral on commercial real estate and commercial and industrial (C&I) loans at a handful of regional banks.

BDC equities have also sold off recently; investors may be more concerned about the interest income generated by the underlying loans amid still-elevated PIK interest expensing arrangements and expectations of lower interest rates.

On top of this, prices of bank loans trading in the secondary market have started to diverge, with the auto and chemicals sectors seeing more loans trading at distressed or discounted levels.

The chemicals sector, which is very exposed to global industrial production cycles and international trade, has seen roughly 20% of loans trade in the range of 80 to 90 cents on the dollar, according to Intex and Markit data. This indicates heightened credit concerns, although not necessarily distress (yet).

Over the past year, consumer fundamentals have also shifted. Although U.S. household balance sheets in aggregate still look very strong, the gaps between different household groups appear to be widening. Labor markets have cooled, real labor income growth has decelerated to a roughly 1% year-over-year pace, and net hiring has ground to a halt, according to U.S. Commerce Department data.

This has coincided with a greater deceleration in nominal wage gains in lower-income jobs, despite still-elevated inflation. Also, the accelerated house price appreciation that supported homeowners (66% of Americans own their primary residence, according to the Census Bureau) has begun to taper off.

Equity market gains are boosting wealth for higher-income households, but relatively few Americans (21%, according to Federal Reserve data) have any exposure to equity markets outside of retirement accounts. Aside from a recent acceleration in spending for high income households, likely supported by equity market gains, U.S. consumer spending appears weak.

The net result of all of these developments appears to be more stress for small to middle-market businesses, lower-income households, and people who don’t own homes.

Delinquencies and defaults are rising more significantly for these groups, despite the dramatic rebound in broad U.S. equity indexes, including the S&P 500, since the initial April tariff announcements – with more stress potentially to come.

Bottom line

Recent bankruptcies along with price movements in some areas of credit and equity markets underscore the widening gap between winners and losers in the U.S. economy.

Although overall corporate and household debt outstanding as a percentage of GDP is lower today than before the pandemic, the mix of lending – which shifted away from banks toward nonbank and private markets with more lenient underwriting standards – creates vulnerabilities, especially amid macroeconomic adjustments likely to follow policy changes.

While we ultimately expect the U.S. economy to strengthen next year, supported by offsetting tax breaks for some, macroeconomic shifts likely won’t be painless for everyone. The pockets of vulnerability mean the outlook for the U.S. economy isn’t without risks.

Broader market pricing appears relatively sanguine about these risks. The S&P 500 is up 15% this year, broader credit yield spreads over Treasuries are still tight (despite some recent repricing), and the shortest-dated interest rates tied to expectations of the path of monetary policy price in very little risk that the Fed will need to cut interest rates more aggressively. We currently anticipate a gradual path of rate cuts, but just how smoothly these economic adjustments will proceed is uncertain.

The upshot of this currently complicated situation is that there are ample opportunities for investors. For example, we favor select investments backed by higher-credit-quality consumer loans and other forms of collateralized lending, when underwritten properly.

Still-elevated yields of high-quality bonds, both in the U.S. and abroad, allow investors to build resilient portfolios while still targeting attractive returns. Elevated yields mean market interest rates don’t have to fall to generate compelling income, while if greater macro risks emerge, price appreciation potential in fixed income may offer an attractive hedge against risk asset underperformance. Across a range of scenarios, bonds should be well-positioned to deliver for investors.
